Maze on the motherboard — my first look inside a computer
One day after school, my dad told me he brought home a computer for me.
"Ray, this is one my company retired. It's yours now." Dad said, half watching TV.

That's right — just like that, I had my own computer. If memory serves, it was Windows 98 (or Windows 95? I'll go with 98 to avoid sounding even older XD). Either way, to me it was just a gaming computer.
"Beep!" the crisp sound after pressing the power button.

Aaah — that familiar sound, the "beep" that got me hooked on computers. Even though I'd been exposed to them early, my access was mostly at my uncle's. The rest of my after-school time was mostly basketball, since smartphones didn't exist then.
Having my own computer was awesome, but I didn't know how to use it. I'd seen Windows 98 in action at relatives' places, but when I sat down to use it myself, I was lost — random clicking everywhere.
GUI = Graphical User Interface — using graphics, images, and visuals to interact with the computer. Unlike traditional text interfaces (like MS-DOS), GUIs make operation way more intuitive.

The interface was unfamiliar, so I just clicked random icons on the desktop. Then I clicked Internet Explorer and finally saw something familiar (what the older cousin had used, from the previous post).
The first page I browsed was...
The computer was a hand-me-down from my dad's company. Their default homepage was Kimo. I typed "
" into the search bar — that's the site the older cousin played games on (I'd watched him do it many times).
Years later, this site is still being updated. Some people still play games there. A few classics I still remember:
Just as I was getting into it, the computer cut to black, broke, shut down. (I'd barely played for any time...)
Wanting to fix it, I decided to try repairing it myself. My dad fixed broken stuff all the time — surely I inherited some of that.
I grabbed a Phillips screwdriver, unscrewed the side panel of the case, opened it up, and looked inside.
"Crap, I can't read any of this! What is all this stuff? It's everywhere..." I thought.

For an elementary school kid who'd just barely started a computer class at school, the inside was a literal maze — winding cables, packed together — no idea what was what, what to remove, where to even start.
So, I sheepishly put the case back on, screwed it back together, grabbed a basketball, and went out to play. (Kids should be running around outside anyway, who needs computers...)
This computer ended up dying entirely later — but that's another story XD (it was basically dying when I got it).
A lot of developers probably don't remember Flash anymore.
Flash stopped updates and support on December 31, 2020.
Flash was Adobe's old multimedia player, widely used for games, animations, and interactive content. Games on "Slime's Game Zone" back then were mostly Flash-built.
"But why can I still play those games?" you might ask.
That ties to why Flash was phased out. Quick summary of the reasons:
Browser tech advanced: HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript (WebGL, HTML5) replaced what Flash did.
Security: Too many vulnerabilities, easy to hack.
UX: Required extra plugins — friction for users.
Those are the main ones — others exist too, but these are the biggies.
decided not to support Flash, which broke a ton of websites and games on iOS. Predictably, developers and users were furious.
Eventually Apple had to bring Flash back. During that period, a lot of people jailbroke their iOS devices to run Flash.
Jailbreak: modifying iOS to remove Apple's restrictions, letting you install unsigned apps and tweak system settings.
This whole saga ended in 2020 when Flash was finally killed, ending that
Have you ever opened your computer to see what's inside?
A lot of people resist opening the case — "
opening is one thing, putting it back together is another
You just need a Phillips screwdriver. If you're tough, you could even use a fingernail!
Shapes vary by manufacturer, but the core layout is similar. There's other stuff too — heatsinks, fans, etc. — but we'll just cover the essentials.
I know motherboard / GPU / CPU sound foreign to beginners, maybe too dense. So here's a table that personifies these parts to make them easier to grok.
Large green or black PCB with sockets and chips
Covers one side of the case interior (usually left)
The skeleton and nervous trunk — fixes and connects all components
Small square chip with metal cap or gold contacts
Upper-center of the motherboard, usually under a fan or heatsink
Logic and computation — the computer's control center
Long stick modules, gold contacts plug into motherboard
Vertical slots next to the CPU
Stores "what you need right now" — wiped on shutdown
Storage bay in case or motherboard M.2 slot
Holds files, OS, apps — persists across reboots
Card with large heatsink and fan
Motherboard's PCIE slot (usually horizontal, lower side)
Metal box with fan and power connectors
Top or bottom corner of case
Converts outlet power and supplies all components
📚 Photo album and old book drawer
Rarely used now — USB or cloud dominates
Motherboard rear network port (RJ-45) or PCIe slot
Talks to the outside world — modern boards usually have this integrated
Simply: a computer is like a person. CPU is the brain, RAM is short-term memory, HDD / SSD is long-term memory, motherboard is the nervous system, PSU is the heart.
For easier identification, here's an image showing what each part looks like:
Maze on the motherboard — annotated internal layout
above aren't absolute — varies by case / motherboard design. But for most builds, this is close to typical.

That's not all — there's also the "north bridge / south bridge". These are two chips on the motherboard that handle communication between components. Like a nervous system, they coordinate the others. Modern motherboards no longer separate north / south — those functions are integrated into the CPU or chipset.

In my school computer class, there was a requirement to earn the "Computer Hardware Technician Class C" license. One day my teacher was showing us how to disassemble and assemble a computer. I didn't have tools handy (a classmate had grabbed them first). Impatient, I used my long fingernails to undo the screws and pop open the case, then smoothly disassembled and reassembled the machine. The teacher was shocked.
Later, the teacher quietly told me...
"You're so good at this — no need to spend money on the cert. How about you help tutor other students instead? I'll just pass you for this class directly."
Just like that, I became the computer-class TA (lol).

Code Review of My Youth on IT Home (in Mandarin)

My dad brought home a computer from his office and my first personal computer was born. From hype to total confusion to it breaking down — I opened the case for the first time and entered the mysterious world called the "motherboard". I started to understand what's inside a computer: CPU, RAM, hard drive, PSU. Every piece was a starting point for my future engineering life.
